summary: Introduces preterite endings for -er/-ir verbs (-í for I, -ió for he/she), teaches que as 'than' for comparisons with más que, introduces nada (nothing), nadie (nobody), sin (without), and durante (during/for). Also covers double negatives and the verb durar.

teachings.conceptName: Preterite tense for -er/-ir verbs (I and he/she)
teachings.summary: For -er/-ir verbs in the preterite: remove ending, add -í for I, -ió for he/she/it/usted. Both endings are accented.
teachings.rule: For -er/-ir verbs: I = -í (comí), he/she/it/usted = -ió (comió). Ver is regular here: vi, vio.

examples.spanish: Lo vi, pero él no me vio
examples.english: I saw him, but he didn't see me

teachings.notes: Ver is regular in the preterite: vi, vio.

teachings.summary: Que means what, that, AND than. For comparisons: más que = more than. After 'than', use subject pronouns (él, yo) not object pronouns, because the meaning is 'more than he ate', 'more than I ate'.
teachings.rule: Más + que = more than. Use subject pronouns after que (él, yo, not him, me).

examples.english: I ate more than him (more than he)
He ate more than me (more than I)

teachings.notes: English uses object pronouns after 'than' (more than him/me) which is actually peculiar. Spanish is more logical: más que él = more than he (ate).

teachings.summary: Nada means nothing. Spanish uses double negatives: no vi nada = I didn't see nothing = I didn't see anything.
teachings.rule: Double negative: no + verb + nada = didn't see anything

teachings.summary: Nadie means nobody. Can be combined with nada in double negative structures.
Durante means during and can also be used for 'for' with time periods, as an alternative to por.
teachings.rule: Durante = during, also works as 'for' with time. Alternative to por for time durations.

examples.english: He ate for half an hour
He spoke for half an hour

examples.spanish: Habló durante media hora sin parar
examples.english: He spoke for half an hour without stopping

teachings.notes: Durante comes from durar (to last) using the -ante adjective form. Can replace por for time periods.

teachings.summary: Sin means without. As a preposition, it takes the infinitive (sin parar = without stopping, not *sin parando).

examples.spanish: Habló durante media hora sin parar
examples.english: He spoke for half an hour without stopping
